name: Fish Pepper
image_alt: Variegated white and green striped peppers on a plant with mottled foliage
description: A striking African-American heirloom pepper with dramatically variegated cream-and-green foliage and fruits that pass through white, striped, and orange stages before ripening red. Heat level is moderate — hotter than a jalapeño, milder than a serrano.
history: Used extensively in 19th-century Baltimore fish and shellfish cookery in African-American communities. Nearly lost, it was preserved by artist and gardener Horace Pippin's family and reintroduced through William Woys Weaver's seed preservation work in the 1990s.

bad.reason: Attracts cabbage loopers which spread to pepper plants

seasonal_quirks: The variegation is most vivid in cooler parts of the season. Fruits go through a beautiful progression of colors — white with green stripes, then orange-striped, then solid red. Each stage has a slightly different flavor profile.
harvest_cues: Harvest at any color stage depending on heat preference — white for mildest, red for hottest. Fruits are 2–3 inches long and slightly curved.
variety_notes: The ornamental quality rivals any flowering plant — grow it where it can be seen. The variegated foliage is a genetic trait; some plants express it more strongly than others.
preservation: Excellent pickled whole at the white stage. Dry red fruits for flakes or powder.
